parts.content:
A point $P$ moves around the unit circle centered at the origin $O(0,0)$ with coordinates $P(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)$.

At a certain instant, the $x$-coordinate of $P$ is $\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$.
Find all possible values of $\sin\theta$.
parts.markscheme:
- From identity $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\Rightarrow \sin^2\theta=1-(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}})^2=\tfrac{9}{10}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin\theta=\pm\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{10}}$. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: If $P$ lies in the fourth quadrant, find the exact values of $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$.
parts.markscheme:
- In quadrant IV: $\sin\theta=-\tfrac{3}{\sqrt{10}},\,\cos\theta=\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2(-\tfrac{3}{\sqrt{10}})(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}})=-\tfrac{6}{10}=-\tfrac{3}{5}.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\cos(2\theta)=2\cos^2\theta-1=2(\tfrac{1}{10})-1=-\tfrac{8}{10}=-\tfrac{4}{5}.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- $\tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)}=\dfrac{-3/5}{-4/5}=\dfrac{3}{4}.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Verify that the identity $\cot^2(2\theta)+1=\csc^2(2\theta)$ holds for your values.
parts.markscheme:
- LHS $=\cot^2(2\theta)+1=(\tfrac{4}{3})^2+1=\tfrac{16}{9}+1=\tfrac{25}{9}.$ <marks>M1</marks>
- RHS $=\csc^2(2\theta)=\dfrac{1}{\sin^2(2\theta)}=\dfrac{1}{(-3/5)^2}=\tfrac{25}{9}.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content:
For this part only, let $\theta$ vary freely.

A quantity $Q$ measured at $P$ is defined by $Q=(\sqrt{2}\sin\theta+\sqrt{2}\cos\theta)^2$.
Determine the maximum and minimum possible values of $Q$, giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Simplify: $Q=(\sqrt{2}(\sin\theta+\cos\theta))^2=2(\sin\theta+\cos\theta)^2=2(1+\sin(2\theta)).$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Max when $\sin(2\theta)=1\Rightarrow Q_\text{max}=4.$ <marks>A1</marks>
- Min when $\sin(2\theta)=-1\Rightarrow Q_\text{min}=0.$ <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Hence find the range of $\theta$ (in radians, $0\le\theta<2\pi$) for which the measured quantity $Q$ is greater than $1$.
parts.markscheme:
- $2(1+\sin(2\theta))>1\Rightarrow 1+\sin(2\theta)>\tfrac{1}{2}\Rightarrow \sin(2\theta)>-\tfrac{1}{2}.$ <marks>M1</marks>
- For $0\le 2\theta<4\pi$, $\sin(2\theta)=-\tfrac{1}{2}\Rightarrow 2\theta=\tfrac{7\pi}{6},\,\tfrac{11\pi}{6},\,\tfrac{19\pi}{6},\,\tfrac{23\pi}{6}.$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Therefore $2\theta$ is outside $[\tfrac{7\pi}{6}, \tfrac{11\pi}{6}]$ and $[\tfrac{19\pi}{6}, \tfrac{23\pi}{6}]$, so $\theta \in [0, \tfrac{7\pi}{12}) \cup (\tfrac{11\pi}{12}, \tfrac{19\pi}{12}) \cup (\tfrac{23\pi}{12}, 2\pi)$. <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content:
A point \(P\) moves around the unit circle centered at the origin \(O(0,0)\) with coordinates \(P(\cos\theta,\sin\theta)\).

At a certain instant, the \(y\)-coordinate of \(P\) is \(\dfrac{12}{13}\).
Find all possible values of \(\cos\theta\).
parts.markscheme:
- From identity \(\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\Rightarrow \cos^2\theta=1-(\tfrac{12}{13})^2=\tfrac{25}{169}\). <marks>M1</marks>
- \(\cos\theta=\pm\dfrac{5}{13}\). <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: If \(P\) lies in the second quadrant, find the exact values of \(\sin(2\theta)\), \(\cos(2\theta)\), and \(\tan(2\theta)\).
parts.markscheme:
- In quadrant II: \(\sin\theta=\tfrac{12}{13},\,\cos\theta=-\tfrac{5}{13}\). <marks>M1</marks>
- \(\sin(2\theta)=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2(\tfrac{12}{13})(-\tfrac{5}{13})=-\tfrac{120}{169}.\) <marks>A1</marks>
- \(\cos(2\theta)=1-2\sin^2\theta=1-2(\tfrac{144}{169})=-\tfrac{119}{169}.\) <marks>A1</marks>
- \(\tan(2\theta)=\dfrac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)}=\dfrac{-120/169}{-119/169}=\dfrac{120}{119}.\) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Verify that the identity \(1+\tan^2(2\theta)=\sec^2(2\theta)\) holds for your values.

A quantity \(Q\) measured at \(P\) is proportional to \((\sqrt{2}\sin\theta-\sqrt{2}\cos\theta)^2\).
Determine the **maximum** and **minimum** possible values of \(Q\), giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Simplify: \(Q \propto 2(\sin\theta-\cos\theta)^2 = 2(1-\sin(2\theta)).\) <marks>M1</marks>
- Max when \(\sin(2\theta)=-1\Rightarrow Q_\text{max}=4.\) <marks>A1</marks>
- Min when \(\sin(2\theta)=1\Rightarrow Q_\text{min}=0.\) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Hence find the range of \(\theta\) (in radians, \(0\le\theta<2\pi\)) for which the measured quantity $Q$ is greater than $3$.
parts.markscheme:
- \(2(1-\sin(2\theta))>3\Rightarrow 1-\sin(2\theta)>1.5\Rightarrow \sin(2\theta)<-0.5.\) <marks>M1</marks>
- \(\sin(2\theta)=-0.5\Rightarrow 2\theta=\dfrac{7\pi}{6},\,\dfrac{11\pi}{6}.\
- Therefore \(2\theta\in(\tfrac{7\pi}{6},\tfrac{11\pi}{6})\cup(\tfrac{19\pi}{6},\tfrac{23\pi}{6})\). <marks>M1</marks>
- Divide by 2: \(\theta\in\Bigl(\tfrac{7\pi}{12},\tfrac{11\pi}{12}\Bigr)\cup\Bigl(\tfrac{19\pi}{12},\tfrac{23\pi}{12}\Bigr).\) <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Find all possible values of $\sin\theta$.
parts.markscheme:
- From identity $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1\Rightarrow \sin^2\theta=1-(\tfrac{1}{\sqrt{10}})^2=\tfrac{9}{10}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin\theta=\pm\dfrac{3}{\sqrt{10}}$. <marks>A1</marks>

questions.specification: Find the measure of angle $L$ in triangle $KLM$ given that $K$ measures $56^{\circ}$, $LM=16.8$ and $KM=13.5$.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.2—2d and 3d trig, sine rule, cosine rule, area > Question Type 3: Finding sides and angles using sine rule
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Finding sides and angles using sine rule
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Given one angle and two sides in a triangle, the natural method to find another angle is the sine rule (using the known angle and its opposite side to find the remaining angles).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.5—Unit circle definitions of sin, cos, tan. Exact trig ratios, ambiguous case of sine rule > Question Type 8: Given the triangle suffers from ambiguous case, finding both possible angles
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 8: Given the triangle suffers from ambiguous case, finding both possible angles
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: With the sine rule and SSA-type data (one angle and two sides), there can be an ambiguous case where two different angles are possible depending on the side lengths; this may need to be considered.

parts.content: Find the measure of angle $L$.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute into Sine Rule: $\frac{\sin L}{13.5}=\frac{\sin 56^{\circ}}{16.8}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Find primary angle: $L = \sin^{-1}\left(\frac{13.5 \times \sin 56^{\circ}}{16.8}\right) \approx 41.8^{\circ}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Consider ambiguous case: $180^{\circ} - 41.8^{\circ} = 138.2^{\circ}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Reject second case since $138.2^{\circ} + 56^{\circ} > 180^{\circ}$ and state final answer: $L \approx 41.8^{\circ}$ <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Identify knowns and unknowns
steps.content:
First, let's match the given information to the sides and angles of the triangle. We use lowercase letters for sides opposite to the corresponding angles.

*   Angle $K = 56^{\circ}$
*   Side opposite $K$ is $LM$, so $k = 16.8$
*   Side opposite $L$ is $KM$, so $l = 13.5$

We need to find the measure of **angle $L$**. Since we have a known "pair" (angle $K$ and side $k$) and are looking for an angle ($L$) corresponding to a known side ($l$), this is a **Sine Rule** problem.

steps.title: Apply the Sine Rule
steps.content:
The Sine Rule states that the ratio of a side length to the sine of its opposite angle is constant for all three pairs in a triangle. 

We use the formula from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet:

$$\frac{a}{\sin A}=\frac{b}{\sin B}=\frac{c}{\sin C}$$

Adapting this for our triangle $KLM$:

$$\frac{k}{\sin K} = \frac{l}{\sin L}$$

steps.title: Substitute and rearrange
steps.content:
Substitute the known values into the equation:

$$\frac{16.8}{\sin 56^{\circ}} = \frac{13.5}{\sin L}$$

Now, rearrange the equation to solve for $\sin L$:

$$\begin{aligned} 16.8 \times \sin L &= 13.5 \times \sin 56^{\circ} \\ \sin L &= \frac{13.5 \times \sin 56^{\circ}}{16.8} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the primary angle
steps.content:
Now we calculate the value of $\sin L$ and use the inverse sine function ($\sin^{-1}$ or $\arcsin$) to find the angle.

$$\sin L \approx \frac{13.5 \times 0.829}{16.8} \approx 0.6662$$

$$L = \sin^{-1}(0.6662) \approx 41.77^{\circ}$$

gdc.instructions:
Ensure you are in Degree mode.
1. Select the trig menu and choose asin or sin⁻¹
2. Input (13.5 × sin(56)) ÷ 16.8
3. Press EXE

ti84.instructions:
Make sure your calculator is in Degree mode (MODE > DEGREE).
1. Press [2nd] then [sin] to get sin⁻¹
2. Type (13.5 * sin(56)) / 16.8
3. Press [ENTER]

desmos.instructions:
Type arcsin((13.5 * sin(56 degrees)) / 16.8)
Ensure the wrench settings are set to Degrees.

calculator.description: Calculate the inverse sine to find angle L

steps.title: Check for ambiguous case
steps.content:
When using the Sine Rule to find an angle, there is a possibility of two solutions (the ambiguous case). The second possible angle is found by subtracting the primary angle from $180^{\circ}$.

$$L_2 = 180^{\circ} - 41.77^{\circ} = 138.23^{\circ}$$

We must check if this second angle is valid. A triangle's angles must sum to $180^{\circ}$. If we add $L_2$ to the given angle $K = 56^{\circ}$:

$$138.23^{\circ} + 56^{\circ} = 194.23^{\circ}$$

Since this sum exceeds $180^{\circ}$, the second case is impossible. Therefore, we reject $138.2^{\circ}$.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Since the ambiguous case is invalid, we have a unique solution. Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$L \approx 41.8^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Setup the Cosine Rule
steps.content:
To find the unknown side $KL$ (let's call it $x$), we apply the Cosine Rule with the known angle $K=56^{\circ}$.

Using the formula $c^2 = a^2 + b^2 - 2ab \cos C$, we assign:
- $LM = 16.8$ (opposite to $K$)
- $KM = 13.5$
- $KL = x$

$$16.8^2 = 13.5^2 + x^2 - 2(13.5)(x) \cos 56^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Form a quadratic equation
steps.content:
Now we simplify the terms to set up a quadratic equation for $x$.

- $16.8^2 = 282.24$
- $13.5^2 = 182.25$
- $2(13.5) \cos 56^{\circ} \approx 15.098$

Substituting these values:
$$282.24 = 182.25 + x^2 - 15.098x$$

Rearranging to the standard form $ax^2 + bx + c = 0$:
$$x^2 - 15.098x - 99.99 = 0$$

gdc.instructions: Enter the calculations directly to find coefficients.

ti84.instructions: Calculate 16.8^2, 13.5^2, and 2*13.5*cos(56). Then subtract 16.8^2 from 13.5^2 to find the constant term.

calculator.description: Calculate the coefficients for the quadratic equation

steps.title: Solve for side KL
steps.content:
Use the quadratic formula or a polynomial solver on your calculator to find $x$.

$$x = \frac{-(-15.098) \pm \sqrt{(-15.098)^2 - 4(1)(-99.99)}}{2}$$

This yields two solutions:
- $x_1 \approx 20.08$
- $x_2 \approx -4.98$

Since a side length must be positive, we accept $KL \approx 20.08$.

gdc.instructions: Use the polynomial solver in the Equation app.

ti84.instructions: Press APPS, select PLYSMLT2, then ROOT FINDER. Enter order 2, then coefficients 1, -15.098, -99.99. Solve.

desmos.instructions: Graph y = x^2 - 15.098x - 99.99 and find the positive x-intercept.

calculator.description: Solve the quadratic equation for x

steps.title: Use Cosine Rule for Angle L
steps.content:
Now that we know all three sides ($KL \approx 20.08$, $LM=16.8$, $KM=13.5$), we use the Cosine Rule again to find angle $L$.

$$KM^2 = LM^2 + KL^2 - 2(LM)(KL) \cos L$$
$$13.5^2 = 16.8^2 + 20.08^2 - 2(16.8)(20.08) \cos L$$

Rearranging for $\cos L$:
$$\cos L = \frac{16.8^2 + 20.08^2 - 13.5^2}{2(16.8)(20.08)}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final angle
steps.content:
Evaluate the expression to find angle $L$.

$$\cos L = \frac{282.24 + 403.206 - 182.25}{674.688} \approx \frac{503.196}{674.688} \approx 0.7458$$

$$L = \cos^{-1}(0.7458) \approx 41.8^{\circ}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the fraction and then take the inverse cosine. Ensure mode is set to DEGREES.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the fraction and then take the inverse cosine. Ensure mode is set to DEGREES.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression. Ensure settings are in Degrees.

questions.specification:
Consider a function $f$, such that $f(x) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + b$, $0 \leq x \leq 10$, $b \in \mathbb{R}$.

The function $f$ has a local maximum at the point $(2, 21.8)$, and a local minimum at $(8, 10.2)$.

A second function $g$ is given by $g(x) = p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right) + q$, $0 \leq x \leq 10$; $p$, $q \in \mathbb{R}$.

The function $g$ passes through the points $(3, 2.5)$ and $(6, 15.1)$.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Given the period and amplitude, finding the trigonometric functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Main task is working with sinusoidal models: determine period from coefficient of x, use max/min values to find vertical shift b, find p and q from points for another sine function, and then compare the two trig functions.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.7—Circular functions, graphs, composites, transformations > Question Type 1: From graphs of trigonometric functions, finding the amplitude and period of the trigonometric curves
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: From graphs of trigonometric functions, finding the amplitude and period of the trigonometric curves
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (a) explicitly asks for the period of f, i.e., extracting period from a trigonometric function (key sinusoid feature).

parts.content: Find the period of $f$.
parts.markscheme:
- Correct approach <marks>M1</marks>
  e.g. $\frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{2\pi}{\text{period}}$ (or equivalent)

- period = 12 <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the angular frequency B
steps.content:
To find the period, we first identify the coefficient $B$ (also known as the angular frequency) from the given function.

The standard form of a sinusoidal function is $y = A\sin(B(x-h)) + k$. Comparing this to the function given in the question:

$$f(x) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + b$$

We can see that the coefficient multiplying the bracket containing $x$ is $B = \frac{\pi}{6}$.

steps.title: Apply the period formula
steps.content:
The period $T$ of a sine or cosine function is calculated using the formula:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{B}$$

Substituting $B = \frac{\pi}{6}$:

$$T = \frac{2\pi}{\frac{\pi}{6}}$$ 

This step demonstrates the correct approach required by the markscheme.

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Now we simplify the expression to find the period:

$$T = 2\pi \times \frac{6}{\pi}$$

$$T = 12$$

The period of the function $f$ is 12.

steps.title: Identify extrema x-coordinates
steps.content:
First, we identify the horizontal positions ($x$-coordinates) of the local maximum and local minimum given in the question.

*   Local maximum occurs at $x = 2$
*   Local minimum occurs at $x = 8$

steps.title: Calculate horizontal distance
steps.content:
Calculate the horizontal distance between the consecutive maximum and minimum. This distance represents half of the full cycle (half-period).

$$\text{Distance} = x_{\text{min}} - x_{\text{max}} = 8 - 2 = 6$$

calculator.description: Calculate the difference between the x-values

steps.title: Calculate the period
steps.content:
Since the distance between a peak (maximum) and a trough (minimum) is exactly half the period, we double this distance to find the full period.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Period} &= 2 \times \text{Distance} \\ &= 2 \times 6 \\ &= 12 \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: Find the value of $b$.
parts.markscheme:
- Valid approach <marks>M1</marks>
  e.g. $\frac{\text{max} + \text{min}}{2}$, $b = \text{max} - \text{amplitude}$
  $\frac{21.8 + 10.2}{2}$, or equivalent

- $b = 16$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the parameter
steps.content:
We need to find the value of $b$. In the given function equation, $b$ represents the **principal axis** (or vertical shift) of the sine wave.

$$f(x) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + b$$

steps.title: Identify extrema values
steps.content:
The question provides the coordinates of the local maximum and minimum. We are interested in their $y$-values.

*   Maximum $y$-value: $21.8$
*   Minimum $y$-value: $10.2$

steps.title: Apply average formula
steps.content:
Using the **Average of Extrema** approach, the principal axis $b$ is found by taking the average of the maximum and minimum $y$-values:

$$b = \frac{y_{\text{max}} + y_{\text{min}}}{2}$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Substitute the values $21.8$ and $10.2$ into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} b &= \frac{21.8 + 10.2}{2} \\ b &= \frac{32}{2} \\ b &= 16 \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter the calculation on the home screen:

calculator.description: Calculate the average of the extrema

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content: The value of $b$ is $16$. This concept is covered in **SL 3.7 Circular functions**.

steps.title: Identify amplitude and maximum value
steps.content:
First, we identify the amplitude and the maximum $y$-value from the given information.

From the function equation $f(x) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + b$, the positive coefficient of the sine function represents the amplitude:
$$\text{Amplitude} = 5.8$$

The question states that the function has a local maximum at a specific point, which gives us the maximum $y$-value:
$$y_{\text{max}} = 21.8$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
For a sinusoidal function of the form $y = A\sin(B(x-C)) + D$, the maximum value is the sum of the vertical translation (principal axis) and the amplitude.

$$y_{\text{max}} = \text{vertical translation} + \text{Amplitude}$$

In this function, $b$ represents the vertical translation (the principal axis). We can set up the equation:
$$21.8 = b + 5.8$$

steps.title: Solve for b
steps.content:
Now, we rearrange the equation to solve for $b$:

$$\begin{aligned} b &= 21.8 - 5.8 \\ b &= 16 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The value of $b$ is:

$$b = 16$$

steps.title: Select a point to substitute
steps.content:
Since the function passes through the local maximum and minimum points, their coordinates satisfy the equation of the function. We can choose either point to substitute into the equation to find $b$. 

Let's use the local maximum point $(2, 21.8)$.

steps.title: Substitute the coordinates
steps.content:
Substitute $x = 2$ and $f(x) = 21.8$ into the given function equation:

$$21.8 = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(2 + 1)\right) + b$$

steps.title: Evaluate the sine term
steps.content:
First, simplify the expression inside the sine function:

$$\frac{\pi}{6}(2 + 1) = \frac{\pi}{6}(3) = \frac{3\pi}{6} = \frac{\pi}{2}$$ 

We know from the unit circle (or calculator) that $\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 1$. The equation becomes:

$$21.8 = 5.8(1) + b$$

gdc.instructions: Ensure angle setting is Radians. Type sin(pi/2) and press EXE

steps.title: Solve for b
steps.content:
Now solve the linear equation for $b$:

$$\begin{aligned} 21.8 &= 5.8 + b \\ b &= 21.8 - 5.8 \\ b &= 16 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The value of $b$ is $16$. 

Note: You would get the same result by substituting the minimum point $(8, 10.2)$, where $\sin\left(\frac{3\pi}{2}\right) = -1$.

parts.content: Hence, find the value of $f(6)$.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to substitute into **their** function <marks>M1</marks>
  $5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(6 + 1)\right) + 16$

- $f(6) = 13.1$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Determine the value of b
steps.content:
First, we need to find the value of $b$, which represents the principal axis (midline) of the function. We can find this using the local maximum value given in the question and the amplitude from the function definition.

The function is $f(x) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + b$. The coefficient $5.8$ is the amplitude.

$$b = \text{maximum value} - \text{amplitude}$$
$$b = 21.8 - 5.8 = 16$$

steps.title: Substitute x = 6 into the function
steps.content:
Now that we know $b=16$, we can write the complete function and substitute $x=6$ to find the required value.

$$f(6) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(6 + 1)\right) + 16$$

steps.title: Evaluate the sine argument
steps.content:
Simplify the expression inside the sine function:

$$\begin{aligned} f(6) &= 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(7)\right) + 16 \\ f(6) &= 5.8\sin\left(\frac{7\pi}{6}\right) + 16 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final value
steps.content:
Evaluate $\sin\left(\frac{7\pi}{6}\right)$. Since $\frac{7\pi}{6}$ is in the third quadrant with a reference angle of $\frac{\pi}{6}$, the sine value is negative.

$$\sin\left(\frac{7\pi}{6}\right) = -0.5$$

Substitute this back into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} f(6) &= 5.8(-0.5) + 16 \\ f(6) &= -2.9 + 16 \\ f(6) &= 13.1 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, set Angle to Radian. Type the expression.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to RADIAN. Enter the expression directly.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression directly into a simplified input line.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: The value of $f(6)$ is **13.1**.

steps.title: Determine the value of b
steps.content:
To plot the function, we first need to determine the value of the vertical shift constant $b$. The function has an amplitude of $5.8$ (the coefficient of the sine term) and a local maximum value of $21.8$.

The maximum value of a sine function is given by $\text{vertical shift} + \text{amplitude}$.

$$\begin{aligned} b + 5.8 &= 21.8 \\ b &= 21.8 - 5.8 \\ b &= 16 \end{aligned}$$

So, the complete function is:
$$f(x) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + 16$$

steps.title: Plot the function
steps.content:
Enter the function into your graphing calculator. Since the angle includes $\pi$, ensure your calculator is set to **RADIANS** mode.

Graph the function:
$$y = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + 16$$

parts.content: Find the value of $p$ and the value of $q$.
parts.markscheme:
- Valid attempt to set up a system of equations <marks>M1</marks>

- Two correct equations <marks>A1</marks>
  $$ \begin{aligned} p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(3 - 3.75)\right) + q &= 2.5 \\ p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(6 - 3.75)\right) + q &= 15.1 \end{aligned} $$

- Valid attempt to solve system <marks>M1</marks>

- $p = 8.4$, $q = 6.7$ <marks>A1</marks> <marks>A1</marks>

5 marks total

steps.title: Strategy: Algebraic System
steps.content:
To find the unknown constants $p$ and $q$, we will create a system of two linear equations. We do this by substituting the $x$ and $y$ coordinates of the two given points into the function $g(x)$.

The function is defined as:
$$g(x) = p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right) + q$$

steps.title: Substitute the first point
steps.content:
First, we substitute the point $(3, 2.5)$ into the function. This means replacing $g(x)$ with $2.5$ and $x$ with $3$.

$$2.5 = p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(3 - 3.75)\right) + q$$

Now, we simplify the expression inside the sine function. Note that $3 - 3.75 = -0.75$ or $-\frac{3}{4}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Angle} &= \frac{2\pi}{9} \times \left(-\frac{3}{4}\right) \\ &= -\frac{6\pi}{36} \\ &= -\frac{\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

Using our knowledge of exact trigonometric ratios (refer to **SL 3.5**), we know that $\sin(-\frac{\pi}{6}) = -0.5$. This gives us our first linear equation:

$$2.5 = -0.5p + q$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value inside the sine function:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value inside the sine function:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value inside the sine function:

calculator.description: Calculate the angle for the first point

steps.title: Substitute the second point
steps.content:
Next, we substitute the point $(6, 15.1)$ into the function.

$$15.1 = p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(6 - 3.75)\right) + q$$

We simplify the angle again. Note that $6 - 3.75 = 2.25$ or $\frac{9}{4}$.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Angle} &= \frac{2\pi}{9} \times \left(\frac{9}{4}\right) \\ &= \frac{18\pi}{36} \\ &= \frac{\pi}{2} \end{aligned}$$

We know that $\sin(\frac{\pi}{2}) = 1$. This gives us our second linear equation:

$$15.1 = 1p + q$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value inside the sine function:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value inside the sine function:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value inside the sine function:

calculator.description: Calculate the angle for the second point

steps.title: Solve the system for p
steps.content:
We now have a system of two linear equations with two unknowns:

$$\begin{aligned} (1) \quad q - 0.5p &= 2.5 \\ (2) \quad q + p &= 15.1 \end{aligned}$$

We can solve this by elimination. Subtract equation (1) from equation (2) to eliminate $q$:

$$\begin{aligned} (q + p) - (q - 0.5p) &= 15.1 - 2.5 \\ 1.5p &= 12.6 \\ p &= \frac{12.6}{1.5} \\ p &= 8.4 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the difference divided by the coefficient:

ti84.instructions: Calculate the difference divided by the coefficient:

desmos.instructions: Calculate the difference divided by the coefficient:

steps.title: Solve for q
steps.content:
Now substitute $p = 8.4$ back into equation (2) to find $q$:

$$\begin{aligned} q + 8.4 &= 15.1 \\ q &= 15.1 - 8.4 \\ q &= 6.7 \end{aligned}$$

Therefore, the values are:

$$p = 8.4, \quad q = 6.7$$

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content:
We need to find the constants $p$ and $q$ for the function $g(x)$. The question provides two points that lie on the curve, which allows us to set up a system of equations.

$$g(x) = p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right) + q$$

highlights.text: Find the value of $p$ and the value of $q$

steps.title: Substitute the first point
steps.content:
The function passes through the point $(3, 2.5)$. Substitute $x = 3$ and $g(3) = 2.5$ into the equation:

$$p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(3 - 3.75)\right) + q = 2.5$$

steps.title: Substitute the second point
steps.content:
The function also passes through the point $(6, 15.1)$. Substitute $x = 6$ and $g(6) = 15.1$ into the equation:

$$p\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(6 - 3.75)\right) + q = 15.1$$

steps.title: Simplify the equations
steps.content:
To make the system easier to enter into a simultaneous equation solver, calculate the values of the sine terms.

For the first equation:
$$\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(3 - 3.75)\right) = \sin\left(-\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = -0.5$$

For the second equation:
$$\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(6 - 3.75)\right) = \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right) = 1$$

steps.title: Solve the system with GDC
steps.content:
Now we have a system of two linear equations with variables $p$ and $q$:

$$\begin{aligned} (1) \quad -0.5p + 1q &= 2.5 \\ (2) \quad 1p + 1q &= 15.1 \end{aligned}$$

Use the simultaneous equation solver on your GDC to find $p$ and $q$.

a1=-0.5, b1=1, c1=2.5
a2=1, b2=1, c2=15.1
Solve.

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
From the solver, we get:

$$p = 8.4$$
$$q = 6.7$$

parts.content: Find the value of $x$ for which the functions have the greatest difference.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to use $|f(x) - g(x)|$ to find maximum difference <marks>M1</marks>

- $x = 1.64$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Define the full functions
steps.content:
First, ensure you have the complete expressions for $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ by determining the constants $b$, $p$, and $q$ using the given points.

For $f(x)$, using the local maximum at $(2, 21.8)$:
$$f(2) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(2 + 1)\right) + b = 21.8 \Rightarrow 5.8(1) + b = 21.8 \Rightarrow b = 16$$

For $g(x)$, using the points $(3, 2.5)$ and $(6, 15.1)$, we solve the system of equations to find $p=8.4$ and $q=6.7$.

Thus, the functions are:
$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + 16 \\ g(x) &= 8.4\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right) + 6.7 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Set up the absolute difference
steps.content:
To find the greatest difference between the functions, we need to maximize the absolute difference $|f(x) - g(x)|$. 

Enter both functions into your graphing calculator and define a third function as the absolute value of their difference.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph mode
2. Enter Y1 and Y2
3. Set Y3 = |Y1 - Y2| using the absolute value key/function

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 and Y2 as the functions f(x) and g(x)
3. For Y3, press [MATH] > [NUM] > [1:abs(]
4. Enter Y1 - Y2 inside the absolute value

desmos.instructions: Type the equation for the absolute difference

calculator.description: Enter the functions and the difference equation

steps.title: Graph and find maximum
steps.content:
Graph the absolute difference function for the domain $0 \leq x \leq 10$. Use the maximum finder tool on your calculator to locate the peak of this graph.

We are looking for the $x$-coordinate where this difference is largest.

gdc.instructions:
1. Draw the graph of Y3
2. Use G-Solve or Analyze Graph > Maximum
3. Select the highest peak in the domain

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [GRAPH]
2. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC)
3. Select [4:maximum]
4. Select Y3 as the curve
5. Set Left Bound, Right Bound, and Guess near the highest peak

desmos.instructions: Click on the highest point of the graph to see its coordinates

calculator.description: Find the maximum of the difference function

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator identifies the maximum difference at approximately $x \approx 1.641$. Rounding to 3 significant figures gives the final answer.

$$x = 1.64$$

steps.title: Define the functions
steps.content:
First, we determine the full equations for $f(x)$ and $g(x)$ using the given information:

For $f(x)$, the local maximum is $21.8$ and local minimum is $10.2$. The vertical shift $b$ is the average of these values:
$$b = \frac{21.8 + 10.2}{2} = 16$$
So, $f(x) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + 16$.

For $g(x)$, substituting the points $(3, 2.5)$ and $(6, 15.1)$ creates a system of equations that yields $p = 8.4$ and $q = 6.7$.
So, $g(x) = 8.4\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right) + 6.7$.

steps.title: Form difference function
steps.content:
To find where the functions have the greatest difference, we need to maximize the absolute difference $|f(x) - g(x)|$. We define the difference function $d(x)$:

$$d(x) = f(x) - g(x)$$
$$d(x) = \left(5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + 16\right) - \left(8.4\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right) + 6.7\right)$$

steps.title: Graph difference function
steps.content: Using a graphing calculator, we graph $y = d(x)$ over the domain $0 \le x \le 10$. We need to identify **both** the local maximum and local minimum coordinates to determine which produces the largest absolute magnitude.

gdc.instructions:
1. In Graph mode, enter y1 = 5.8sin(π/6(x+1)) + 16 - (8.4sin(2π/9(x-3.75)) + 6.7)
2. Set View Window to x: [0, 10]
3. Draw the graph

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = 5.8sin(π/6(X+1)) + 16 - (8.4sin(2π/9(X-3.75)) + 6.7)
3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=10
4. Press [GRAPH]

desmos.instructions:
1. Type f(x) = 5.8sin(pi/6(x+1)) + 16
2. Type g(x) = 8.4sin(2pi/9(x-3.75)) + 6.7
3. Type d(x) = f(x) - g(x)
4. Inspect the graph between x=0 and x=10

calculator.description: Graph the difference function to find extrema

steps.title: Identify local extrema
steps.content:
Using the calculator's maximum and minimum tools, we find the coordinates of the turning points on the graph of $d(x)$:

*   **Local Maximum:** The graph reaches a high peak at approx $x \approx 1.64$. 
    Coordinates: $(1.64, 23.4)$

*   **Local Minimum:** The graph dips to a minimum at approx $x \approx 6.60$.
    Coordinates: $(6.60, -2.68)$

gdc.instructions:
1. Use G-Solve (F5)
2. Select MAX -> x ≈ 1.64, y ≈ 23.4
3. Select MIN -> x ≈ 6.60, y ≈ -2.68

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC)
2. Select 4:maximum, set bounds around the peak, press [ENTER] -> x ≈ 1.64
3. Select 3:minimum, set bounds around the valley, press [ENTER] -> x ≈ 6.60

desmos.instructions: Click on the peak and valley points of the graph to see their coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the coordinates of the local maximum and minimum

steps.title: Compare magnitudes
steps.content:
We compare the absolute values of the y-coordinates to find the greatest difference:

*   At local maximum: $|23.4| = 23.4$
*   At local minimum: $|-2.68| = 2.68$

The value $23.4$ is clearly larger. Therefore, the greatest difference occurs at the local maximum.

steps.title: State the answer
steps.content:
The value of $x$ for which the functions have the greatest difference is $1.64$.

$$x = 1.64$$

steps.title: Define the functions
steps.content:
From the previous parts of the question, we have determined the values of the parameters for $f(x)$ and $g(x)$.

For $f(x)$, the vertical shift $b$ is the midpoint of the maximum and minimum values:
$$b = \frac{21.8 + 10.2}{2} = 16$$

So, $$f(x) = 5.8\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) + 16$$

For $g(x)$, substituting the points $(3, 2.5)$ and $(6, 15.1)$ gives $p = 8.4$ and $q = 6.7$.

So, $$g(x) = 8.4\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right) + 6.7$$

steps.title: Identify optimization condition
steps.content:
We want to find the value of $x$ where the difference between the two functions is greatest. Let $D(x) = f(x) - g(x)$.

The maximum difference occurs when the rate of change of the difference is zero, meaning the derivatives of the two functions are equal:
$$D'(x) = f'(x) - g'(x) = 0 \implies f'(x) = g'(x)$$

steps.title: Find the derivatives
steps.content:
Using the chain rule $\frac{d}{dx}(\sin(ax+b)) = a\cos(ax+b)$, we differentiate both functions.

For $f(x)$:
$$f'(x) = 5.8\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right)$$

For $g(x)$:
$$g'(x) = 8.4\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}\right)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right)$$

steps.title: Solve for x
steps.content:
We equate the derivatives to find the critical point:

$$5.8\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right)\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}(x + 1)\right) = 8.4\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}\right)\cos\left(\frac{2\pi}{9}(x - 3.75)\right)$$

Use a graphing calculator to find the value of $x$ where these two expressions intersect (or where their difference is zero) within the domain $0 \leq x \leq 10$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu.
2. Enter f1(x) = derivative of f(x) and f2(x) = derivative of g(x).
3. Analyze Graph -> Intersection.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = (5.8*pi/6)*cos(pi/6*(X+1)).
3. Enter Y2 = (8.4*2*pi/9)*cos(2*pi/9*(X-3.75)).
4. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select 5:intersect.
5. Move cursor near x = 1.6 and press [ENTER] three times.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type f'(x) expression.
2. Type g'(x) expression.
3. Click the intersection point near x = 1.64.

calculator.description: Find the root of the derivative difference equation

steps.title: State the solution
steps.content:
The calculator yields the solution:
$$x \approx 1.64$$

We can verify this gives the maximum difference by checking the value of $|f(1.64) - g(1.64)|$, which is approximately $23.4$, confirming it is the maximum difference in the domain.

questions.specification:
Let $\sin 20^{\circ}=p$ and $\cos 20^{\circ}=q$ (so that $p^{2}+q^{2}=1$). By observing the unit circle express the following in terms of $p$ and/or $q$.
![](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/c8e870f6-5654-46e9-9e56-cf78411483d8.png)

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Geometry & Trigonometry > SL 3.5—Unit circle definitions of sin, cos, tan. Exact trig ratios, ambiguous case of sine rule > Question Type 1: Determining relationship between trigonometric functions using unit circle
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 1: Determining relationship between trigonometric functions using unit circle
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Uses unit-circle/quadrant symmetries to rewrite sin, cos, tan of 160° and 200° in terms of sin 20°=p and cos 20°=q, focusing on sign changes and reference angles.

parts.content: $\sin 160^{\circ}$
parts.markscheme:
- Since $\sin 160^{\circ}=\sin 20^{\circ}$, then $\sin 160^{\circ}=p$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Locate angle in Quadrant II
steps.content: First, we locate the angle $160^{\circ}$ on the unit circle. Since $160^{\circ}$ is between $90^{\circ}$ and $180^{\circ}$, the terminal side of the angle lies in the **second quadrant**.

steps.title: Identify the reference angle
steps.content:
To use symmetry, we need to find the **reference angle**, which is the acute angle made with the horizontal axis (the $x$-axis).

Since our angle is $160^{\circ}$, we calculate how far it is from $180^{\circ}$:
$$180^{\circ} - 160^{\circ} = 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Compare vertical displacements
steps.content:
On the unit circle, the sine of an angle corresponds to its vertical displacement ($y$-coordinate). 

Looking at the symmetry between Quadrant I and Quadrant II:
- The angle $20^{\circ}$ is at a certain height above the $x$-axis.
- The angle $160^{\circ}$ is at the exact same height above the $x$-axis.

Therefore, their vertical displacements are identical, meaning their sine values are equal:
$$\sin 160^{\circ} = \sin 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The question defines $\sin 20^{\circ} = p$. Since we found that $\sin 160^{\circ}$ is equal to $\sin 20^{\circ}$, we can express the answer directly in terms of $p$:

$$\sin 160^{\circ} = p$$

steps.title: Analyze angle relationship
steps.content:
First, notice that the angle $160^{\circ}$ is in the second quadrant. It is related to $20^{\circ}$ because they are supplementary angles (they add up to $180^{\circ}$):

$$160^{\circ} = 180^{\circ} - 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply supplementary angle identity
steps.content:
We use the **supplementary angle identity** for sine, which states that the sine of an angle is equal to the sine of its supplement. This is a fundamental property of the unit circle (Concept **SL 3.5**).

$$\sin(180^{\circ} - \theta) = \sin \theta$$

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Substitute $\theta = 20^{\circ}$ into the identity:

$$\sin(160^{\circ}) = \sin(180^{\circ} - 20^{\circ}) = \sin 20^{\circ}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Run-Matrix mode.
2. Ensure angle unit is set to Degrees in Setup.
3. Type 'sin(160)' and press [EXE].
4. Type 'sin(20)' and press [EXE].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and ensure Degree mode is selected.
2. Press [SIN] [1] [6] [0] [)] [ENTER].
3. Press [SIN] [2] [0] [)] [ENTER].
4. Compare the values.

desmos.instructions:
1. Type 'sin(160 degrees)'.
2. Type 'sin(20 degrees)'.
3. Verify the decimal values are identical.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The question defines $\sin 20^{\circ} = p$. Therefore:

$$\sin 160^{\circ} = p$$

parts.content: $\cos 160^{\circ}$
parts.markscheme:
- Since $\cos 160^{\circ}=-\cos 20^{\circ}$, then $\cos 160^{\circ}=-q$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Locate the angle
steps.content:
First, let's locate the angle $160^{\circ}$ on the unit circle. 

Since $90^{\circ} < 160^{\circ} < 180^{\circ}$, the terminal side of the angle lies in the **second quadrant**.

steps.title: Find the reference angle
steps.content:
In the second quadrant, the reference angle is the acute angle made with the negative $x$-axis. We calculate this by subtracting the angle from $180^{\circ}$:

$$180^{\circ} - 160^{\circ} = 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Determine the sign using symmetry
steps.content:
On the unit circle, the cosine of an angle corresponds to the **$x$-coordinate**.

In the second quadrant, the $x$-coordinates are negative. By symmetry, $\cos 160^{\circ}$ will have the same magnitude as the cosine of its reference angle ($20^{\circ}$), but with a negative sign.

Therefore:
$$\cos 160^{\circ} = -\cos 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Substitute the given value
steps.content:
The question specifies the value for the reference angle. Substituting $q$ for $\cos 20^{\circ}$ gives:

$$\cos 160^{\circ} = -q$$

steps.title: Express the angle using 180°
steps.content:
To evaluate $\cos 160^{\circ}$, we first recognize that $160^{\circ}$ is in the second quadrant. We can express this angle using the supplementary angle relationship with $180^{\circ}$:

$$160^{\circ} = 180^{\circ} - 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply supplementary angle identity
steps.content:
Using the supplementary angle identity $\cos(180^{\circ} - \theta) = -\cos \theta$ with $\theta = 20^{\circ}$, we get:

$$\cos 160^{\circ} = \cos(180^{\circ} - 20^{\circ}) = -\cos 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Substitute the given variable
steps.content:
The question defines $\cos 20^{\circ} = q$. Substituting this into our expression:

$$\cos 160^{\circ} = -q$$

parts.content: $\tan 160^{\circ}$
parts.markscheme:
- Since $\tan 160^{\circ}=-\tan 20^{\circ}$, then $\tan 160^{\circ}=-\frac{p}{q}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify coordinates for 20°
steps.content:
First, let's establish the coordinates for $20^{\circ}$ on the unit circle. 

We know that for any angle $\theta$ on the unit circle, the coordinates are $(x, y) = (\cos \theta, \sin \theta)$.

Given that $\cos 20^{\circ} = q$ and $\sin 20^{\circ} = p$, the point corresponding to $20^{\circ}$ is:
$$(q, p)$$

steps.title: Locate 160° on the unit circle
steps.content:
Next, let's find the position of $160^{\circ}$. 

Since $160^{\circ} = 180^{\circ} - 20^{\circ}$, this angle is in the second quadrant. Geometrically, the point for $160^{\circ}$ is the reflection of the point for $20^{\circ}$ across the $y$-axis.

steps.title: Find coordinates for 160°
steps.content:
When reflecting a point across the $y$-axis, the $x$-coordinate changes sign (becomes negative) while the $y$-coordinate stays the same.

*   Original coordinates ($20^{\circ}$): $(q, p)$
*   Reflected coordinates ($160^{\circ}$): $(-q, p)$

So, for $160^{\circ}$, we have $x = -q$ and $y = p$.

steps.title: Calculate tan 160°
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the tangent using the definition $\tan \theta = \frac{y}{x}$.

Substituting our coordinates for $160^{\circ}$:
$$\tan 160^{\circ} = \frac{y}{x} = \frac{p}{-q}$$ 

Therefore:
$$\tan 160^{\circ} = -\frac{p}{q}$$

steps.title: Find quadrant and reference angle
steps.content:
First, we locate $160^{\circ}$ on the unit circle. Since $90^{\circ} < 160^{\circ} < 180^{\circ}$, the angle is in the **second quadrant**.

The reference angle is the acute angle formed with the x-axis:
$$180^{\circ} - 160^{\circ} = 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply the Reference Angle Rule
steps.content:
Next, we determine the sign of the tangent function in the second quadrant. Using the **CAST rule** (or ASTC), we know that in the second quadrant:
- Sine is positive
- Cosine is negative
- **Tangent is negative**

Therefore, applying the reference angle rule:
$$\tan 160^{\circ} = -\tan 20^{\circ}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate tan(160) and -tan(20) to confirm they are equal.

ti84.instructions: Calculate tan(160) and -tan(20) to confirm they are equal.

desmos.instructions: Type tan(160 deg) and -tan(20 deg) to see they yield the same value.

calculator.description: Verify the relationship between tan(160) and -tan(20)

steps.title: Express in terms of p and q
steps.content:
Now we substitute the values given in the question. We know the fundamental identity for tangent is:
$$\tan \theta = \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}$$

Substituting $\theta = 20^{\circ}$:
$$\tan 20^{\circ} = \frac{\sin 20^{\circ}}{\cos 20^{\circ}}$$

Using the given values $p$ and $q$:
$$\tan 20^{\circ} = \frac{p}{q}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Combining our results from the previous steps:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan 160^{\circ} &= -\tan 20^{\circ} \\ &= -\left( \frac{p}{q} \right) \\ &= -\frac{p}{q} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Rewrite the angle
steps.content:
To evaluate $\tan 160^{\circ}$, we first express the angle $160^{\circ}$ in terms of $180^{\circ}$ and $20^{\circ}$ to use the compound angle formula.

$$160^{\circ} = 180^{\circ} - 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: State the compound angle formula
steps.content:
We apply the compound angle formula for tangent, $\tan(A - B)$.

$$\tan(A - B) = \frac{\tan A - \tan B}{1 + \tan A \tan B}$$

In this case, let $A = 180^{\circ}$ and $B = 20^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Evaluate the components
steps.content:
Next, we find the values of $\tan 180^{\circ}$ and $\tan 20^{\circ}$.

From the unit circle, we know that at $180^{\circ}$ the sine is 0 and cosine is -1, so:
$$\tan 180^{\circ} = 0$$

Given that $\sin 20^{\circ} = p$ and $\cos 20^{\circ} = q$, we can express $\tan 20^{\circ}$ as:
$$\tan 20^{\circ} = \frac{\sin 20^{\circ}}{\cos 20^{\circ}} = \frac{p}{q}$$

gdc.instructions: Enter tan(160) and -tan(20) to confirm they are equal.

ti84.instructions: Enter tan(160) and -tan(20) to confirm they are equal.

desmos.instructions: Type tan(160 deg) and -tan(20 deg) to verify equality.

calculator.description: Verify the relationship between tan(160) and -tan(20)

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
Now we substitute these values into the formula:

$$\tan(160^{\circ}) = \frac{0 - \frac{p}{q}}{1 + 0 \cdot \frac{p}{q}} = -\frac{p}{q}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Therefore, the expression in terms of $p$ and $q$ is:

$$-\frac{p}{q}$$

parts.content: $\sin 200^{\circ}$
parts.markscheme:
- Since $\sin 200^{\circ}=-\sin 20^{\circ}$, then $\sin 200^{\circ}=-p$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Locate the angle
steps.content:
First, we need to determine which quadrant the angle $200^\circ$ lies in on the unit circle. 

We know that:
*   $0^\circ$ to $90^\circ$ is Quadrant 1
*   $90^\circ$ to $180^\circ$ is Quadrant 2
*   $180^\circ$ to $270^\circ$ is Quadrant 3

Since $200^\circ$ is between $180^\circ$ and $270^\circ$, the angle is in the **third quadrant**.

steps.title: Find the reference angle
steps.content:
Next, we find the reference angle, which is the acute angle formed between the terminal side of $200^\circ$ and the x-axis (specifically $180^\circ$).

$$\text{Reference Angle} = 200^\circ - 180^\circ = 20^\circ$$

This tells us that the value of $\sin 200^\circ$ will be related to $\sin 20^\circ$ by symmetry. This relates to **Topic 3.5: Unit circle definitions**.

gdc.instructions: Ensure angle unit is degrees. Calculate sin(200) and -sin(20).

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to DEGREE. Calculate sin(200) and -sin(20).

calculator.description: Calculate sine of 200 degrees and sine of 20 degrees to verify the relationship.

steps.title: Apply sign based on quadrant
steps.content:
In the unit circle, the sine function corresponds to the y-coordinate. In the third quadrant, y-coordinates are negative.

Therefore, the sine of $200^\circ$ is the negative of the sine of its reference angle $20^\circ$:

$$\sin 200^\circ = -\sin 20^\circ$$

steps.title: Substitute the given value
steps.content:
The question defines $\sin 20^\circ = p$. Substituting this into our equation gives the final answer in terms of $p$:

$$\sin 200^\circ = -p$$

steps.title: Express angle as a sum
steps.content:
To find $\sin 200^{\circ}$, we can express the angle $200^{\circ}$ as a sum involving $180^{\circ}$:

$$200^{\circ} = 180^{\circ} + 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply compound angle identity
steps.content:
We use the compound angle identity for sine, $\sin(A + B)$, setting $A = 180^{\circ}$ and $B = 20^{\circ}$:

$$\sin(A + B) = \sin A \cos B + \cos A \sin B$$

steps.title: Substitute known values
steps.content:
From the unit circle, we know that $\sin 180^{\circ} = 0$ and $\cos 180^{\circ} = -1$. We also use the given values $\sin 20^{\circ} = p$ and $\cos 20^{\circ} = q$. Substituting these into the expansion:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(180^{\circ} + 20^{\circ}) &= \sin 180^{\circ} \cos 20^{\circ} + \cos 180^{\circ} \sin 20^{\circ} \\ &= (0)(q) + (-1)(p) \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Press trig key, select sin, type 180, ENTER -> 0
2. Press trig key, select cos, type 180, ENTER -> -1

ti84.instructions:
Ensure mode is set to DEGREE.
1. Type sin(180) and press ENTER -> 0
2. Type cos(180) and press ENTER -> -1

calculator.description: Verify the values of sin(180) and cos(180)

steps.title: Simplify final expression
steps.content:
Simplifying the expression gives our result in terms of $p$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin 200^{\circ} &= 0 - p \\ &= -p \end{aligned}$$

parts.content: $\cos 200^{\circ}$
parts.markscheme:
- Since $\cos 200^{\circ}=-\cos 20^{\circ}$, then $\cos 200^{\circ}=-q$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify the quadrant and reference angle
steps.content:
First, we locate the angle $200^{\circ}$ on the unit circle. Since $180^{\circ} < 200^{\circ} < 270^{\circ}$, the angle is in the third quadrant.

We can express $200^{\circ}$ relative to the horizontal axis ($180^{\circ}$):
$$200^{\circ} = 180^{\circ} + 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply unit circle symmetry
steps.content:
On the unit circle, the cosine value corresponds to the $x$-coordinate. In the third quadrant, the $x$-coordinates are negative.

By symmetry, the cosine of $180^{\circ} + \theta$ corresponds to the negative of the cosine of the reference angle $\theta$:
$$\cos(180^{\circ} + 20^{\circ}) = -\cos 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Substitute the given variable
steps.content:
The question defines $\cos 20^{\circ}=q$. We substitute this into our expression:

$$\cos 200^{\circ} = -q$$

steps.title: Decompose the angle
steps.content:
To evaluate $\cos 200^{\circ}$ using the compound angle identity, we first rewrite the angle $200^{\circ}$ as a sum involving $180^{\circ}$:

$$200^{\circ} = 180^{\circ} + 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Apply compound angle identity
steps.content:
We use the cosine compound angle formula, which states that $\cos(A + B) = \cos A \cos B - \sin A \sin B$. Setting $A = 180^{\circ}$ and $B = 20^{\circ}$, we get:

$$\cos(180^{\circ} + 20^{\circ}) = \cos 180^{\circ} \cos 20^{\circ} - \sin 180^{\circ} \sin 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Determine values for 180°
steps.content:
Using the unit circle, we identify the values for $\cos 180^{\circ}$ and $\sin 180^{\circ}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos 180^{\circ} &= -1 \\ \sin 180^{\circ} &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute and simplify
steps.content:
We substitute these values into our equation. The question defines $\sin 20^{\circ}=p$ and $\cos 20^{\circ}=q$:

$$\cos 200^{\circ} = (-1)(q) - (0)(p) = -q$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The expression for $\cos 200^{\circ}$ in terms of $p$ and/or $q$ is:

$$-q$$

parts.content: $\tan 200^{\circ}$

- Since $\tan 200^{\circ}=\tan 20^{\circ}$, then $\tan 200^{\circ}=\frac{p}{q}$ <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Locate angle on unit circle
steps.content:
First, we locate the angle $200^{\circ}$ on the unit circle. Since $180^{\circ} < 200^{\circ} < 270^{\circ}$, the angle lies in the **third quadrant**.

The reference angle with the x-axis is:
$$200^{\circ} - 180^{\circ} = 20^{\circ}$$

steps.title: Identify the coordinates
steps.content:
On the unit circle, any point is defined by coordinates $(x, y) = (\cos \theta, \sin \theta)$.

- At $20^{\circ}$ (Quadrant I), the coordinates are $(q, p)$.
- At $200^{\circ}$ (Quadrant III), the point is reflected through the origin (diametrically opposite), so both signs become negative.

So for $200^{\circ}$, the coordinates are:
$$\begin{aligned} x &= -q \\ y &= -p \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the ratio
steps.content:
Using the definition $\tan \theta = \frac{y}{x}$, we substitute the coordinates for $200^{\circ}$:

$$\tan 200^{\circ} = \frac{-p}{-q}$$

Simplifying the negative signs gives:

$$\tan 200^{\circ} = \frac{p}{q}$$

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: We need to express $\tan 200^{\circ}$ in terms of $p$ and $q$, where $\sin 20^{\circ} = p$ and $\cos 20^{\circ} = q$.

steps.title: Apply tangent periodicity
steps.content:
The tangent function is periodic with a period of $180^{\circ}$. This means that for any angle $\theta$, the relationship $\tan(180^{\circ} + \theta) = \tan \theta$ holds true.

We can rewrite $200^{\circ}$ as $180^{\circ} + 20^{\circ}$:

$$\tan 200^{\circ} = \tan(180^{\circ} + 20^{\circ}) = \tan 20^{\circ}$$

gdc.instructions: In Run-Matrix mode, set Angle to Degree. Verify tan(200) = tan(20).

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to Degree. Calculate tan(200) and tan(20) to confirm they are equal.

desmos.instructions: Type tan(200 degrees) and tan(20 degrees) to verify equality.

calculator.description: Verify the periodicity of the tangent function numerically

steps.title: Express in terms of sine and cosine
steps.content:
Using the definition $\tan \theta = \frac{\sin \theta}{\cos \theta}$, we can write:

$$\tan 20^{\circ} = \frac{\sin 20^{\circ}}{\cos 20^{\circ}}$$ Since we found earlier that $\tan 200^{\circ} = \tan 20^{\circ}$, this is also the expression for $\tan 200^{\circ}$.

steps.title: Substitute p and q
steps.content:
Finally, substitute the given values $p = \sin 20^{\circ}$ and $q = \cos 20^{\circ}$ into the expression:

$$\tan 200^{\circ} = \frac{p}{q}$$

parts.content: At a specific geographical coordinate, the $\cos\theta$ component of the heading is known to be $-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. Find all possible values of the $\sin\theta$ component.
parts.markscheme:
- Using $\sin^2\theta+\cos^2\theta=1$, we find $\sin^2\theta=1-\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2=\frac{1}{4}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin\theta=\pm\frac{1}{2}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Select the Pythagorean identity
steps.content: To find $\sin\theta$ when $\cos\theta$ is known, we use the Pythagorean identity from the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet (Topic SL 3.6).

steps.title: Substitute the value
steps.content:
Substitute the given value $\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ into the identity:

$$\sin^2\theta + \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 = 1$$

steps.title: Solve for sin squared
steps.content:
Calculate the square of the cosine term and subtract it from 1:

$$\begin{aligned} \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 &= \frac{3}{4} \\ \sin^2\theta &= 1 - \frac{3}{4} \\ \sin^2\theta &= \frac{1}{4} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions:
1. Enter the expression to verify the fraction: (1 - (-√(3)/2)^2)
2. Convert result to fraction if needed

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to verify the value

calculator.description: Verify the calculation of the squared term and subtraction

steps.title: Find the final values
steps.content:
Take the square root of both sides. Since the question asks for **all possible values** and we do not know the specific quadrant, we must include both the positive and negative roots:

$$\sin\theta = \pm\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}}$$ 

$$\sin\theta = \pm\frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Identify the reference angle
steps.content:
First, we ignore the negative sign to find the **reference angle**. We look for an acute angle $\alpha$ such that $\cos \alpha = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

From our knowledge of exact trigonometric ratios (often memorized from the unit circle or special triangles), we know:

$$\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

So, our reference angle is $\frac{\pi}{6}$ (or $30^\circ$).

steps.title: Determine the correct quadrants
steps.content:
The problem states that the cosine component is **negative**:

$$\cos \theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

On the unit circle, the $x$-coordinate represents cosine. The $x$-values are negative in the **second quadrant (II)** and **third quadrant (III)**. Therefore, our angle $\theta$ must be in one of these two quadrants.

steps.title: Find the angles in quadrants
steps.content:
Using our reference angle of $\frac{\pi}{6}$, we find the specific angles in these quadrants:

**Quadrant II:** $\pi - \text{reference angle}$
$$\theta_1 = \pi - \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{5\pi}{6}$$

**Quadrant III:** $\pi + \text{reference angle}$
$$\theta_2 = \pi + \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{7\pi}{6}$$

steps.title: Evaluate sine for these angles
steps.content:
Now we find the sine value (the $y$-coordinate) for each angle:

For $\theta_1 = \frac{5\pi}{6}$ (Quadrant II), sine is positive:
$$\sin\left(\frac{5\pi}{6}\right) = \sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = \frac{1}{2}$$

For $\theta_2 = \frac{7\pi}{6}$ (Quadrant III), sine is negative:
$$\sin\left(\frac{7\pi}{6}\right) = -\sin\left(\frac{\pi}{6}\right) = -\frac{1}{2}$$

gdc.instructions: Check the sine values of the angles found.

ti84.instructions: Ensure mode is set to RADIAN. Calculate sine of both angles.

desmos.instructions: Type the expressions to verify the values.

calculator.description: Verify the sine values for the calculated angles

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
The possible values for the $\sin\theta$ component are $\frac{1}{2}$ and $-\frac{1}{2}$.

Therefore:
$$\sin\theta = \pm\frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Construct a reference triangle
steps.content:
To find the magnitude of the sine component, we first construct a right-angled triangle based on the given cosine value. We ignore the negative sign for now and use the reference angle $\alpha$ where $\cos \alpha = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

Since $$\cos \alpha = \frac{\text{adjacent}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$$

We draw a triangle with:
*   **Adjacent side** = $\sqrt{3}$
*   **Hypotenuse** = $2$

steps.title: Calculate the opposite side
steps.content:
Next, we use the Pythagorean theorem ($$a^2 + b^2 = c^2$$) to find the length of the opposite side ($b$).

$$\begin{aligned} (\sqrt{3})^2 + b^2 &= 2^2 \\ 3 + b^2 &= 4 \\ b^2 &= 1 \\ b &= 1 \end{aligned}$$

So, the **Opposite side** is $1$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the square root of the difference of squares

ti84.instructions: Calculate the square root of the difference of squares

desmos.instructions: Calculate the square root of the difference of squares

calculator.description: Verify the calculation for the opposite side

steps.title: Find the magnitude of sine
steps.content:
Now we can determine the magnitude (absolute value) of $\sin \theta$ using the definition $$\sin \alpha = \frac{\text{opposite}}{\text{hypotenuse}}$$.

Substituting our values:
$$|\sin \theta| = \frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Determine signs using CAST rule
steps.content:
We are given that $\cos \theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$, which is a negative value. Using the **CAST rule** (or Unit Circle quadrants), cosine is negative in the **2nd** and **3rd** quadrants.

*   In **Quadrant 2**, sine is positive ($+$).
*   In **Quadrant 3**, sine is negative ($-$).

Therefore, $\sin \theta$ can be either positive or negative.

steps.title: State the final values
steps.content:
Combining the magnitude $\frac{1}{2}$ with the possible signs, the possible values for the $\sin \theta$ component are:

$$\sin \theta = \pm \frac{1}{2}$$

parts.content: If the heading lies in the third quadrant, compute the exact values of the double angle terms: $\sin(2\theta)$, $\cos(2\theta)$, and $\tan(2\theta)$.
parts.markscheme:
- In quadrant III: $\sin\theta=-\frac{1}{2}, \, \cos\theta=-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. <marks>M1</marks>
- $\sin(2\theta)=2\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)=\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$. <marks>A1</marks>
- $\cos(2\theta)=\cos^2\theta-\sin^2\theta=\frac{3}{4}-\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{2}$. <marks>A1</marks>
- $\tan(2\theta)=\sqrt{3}$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
From the problem context (Part 1) and the markscheme, we know the heading lies in the third quadrant with the following specific values:

$$\sin\theta = -\frac{1}{2}, \quad \cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

steps.title: Calculate sin(2θ)
steps.content:
We calculate $\sin(2\theta)$ using the double angle identity from the data booklet. Substitute the values of $\sin\theta$ and $\cos\theta$:

$$\begin{aligned} \sin(2\theta) &= 2\sin\theta\cos\theta \\ &= 2\left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)\left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right) \\ &= 2\left(\frac{\sqrt{3}}{4}\right) \\ &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate cos(2θ)
steps.content:
Next, we calculate $\cos(2\theta)$ using its double angle identity from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} \cos(2\theta) &= \cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta \\ &= \left(-\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}\right)^2 - \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2 \\ &= \frac{3}{4} - \frac{1}{4} \\ &= \frac{2}{4} \\ &= \frac{1}{2} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate tan(2θ)
steps.content:
Finally, we find $\tan(2\theta)$ using the ratio identity $\tan(A) = \frac{\sin(A)}{\cos(A)}$, substituting our results from the previous steps:

$$\begin{aligned} \tan(2\theta) &= \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} \\ &= \frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}} \\ &= \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \times \frac{2}{1} \\ &= \sqrt{3} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Identify the coordinate values
steps.content:
To use the **Finding the Angle Value** approach, we first need the specific coordinates of the heading vector. Based on the problem context (or previous part), we have the values:

$$\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \sin\theta = -\frac{1}{2}$$

Since both coordinates are negative, the angle $\theta$ is in the **third quadrant**.

highlights.text: heading lies in the third quadrant

steps.title: Determine the reference angle
steps.content:
First, we find the **reference angle** $\alpha$ by ignoring the negative signs. We look for an acute angle where $\sin\alpha = \frac{1}{2}$ and $\cos\alpha = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$.

From our knowledge of the unit circle (or special triangles):
$$\alpha = 30^\circ \quad \text{or} \quad \frac{\pi}{6} \text{ radians}$$

gdc.instructions: Use trig functions to find inverse sine of 0.5.

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [SIN] (asin), enter 0.5, and press [ENTER]. Ensure mode is set to Degree or Radian as needed.

calculator.description: Find the reference angle using arcsin

steps.title: Find the specific angle θ
steps.content:
Since $\theta$ is in the third quadrant, we add the reference angle to $180^\circ$ (or $\pi$).

$$\begin{aligned} \theta &= 180^\circ + 30^\circ = 210^\circ \\ \text{or} \\ \theta &= \pi + \frac{\pi}{6} = \frac{7\pi}{6} \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: heading lies in the third quadrant

steps.title: Calculate the double angle 2θ
steps.content:
Now we compute the double angle $2\theta$:

$$2\theta = 2 \times 210^\circ = 420^\circ$$

or in radians:
$$2\theta = 2 \times \frac{7\pi}{6} = \frac{7\pi}{3}$$

steps.title: Find the coterminal angle
steps.content:
The angle $420^\circ$ is greater than a full circle ($360^\circ$). To find its equivalent position on the unit circle (coterminal angle), we subtract $360^\circ$:

$$420^\circ - 360^\circ = 60^\circ$$

In radians:
$$\frac{7\pi}{3} - 2\pi = \frac{7\pi}{3} - \frac{6\pi}{3} = \frac{\pi}{3}$$

So, evaluating trig ratios for $2\theta$ is the same as evaluating them for **$60^\circ$ (or $\pi/3$)**.

steps.title: Evaluate the trigonometric ratios
steps.content:
Finally, we calculate the required values using the standard ratios for $60^\circ$ ($\pi/3$):

**1. Sine:**
$$\sin(2\theta) = \sin(60^\circ) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$$

**2. Cosine:**
$$\cos(2\theta) = \cos(60^\circ) = \frac{1}{2}$$

**3. Tangent:**
$$\tan(2\theta) = \tan(60^\circ) = \frac{\sin(60^\circ)}{\cos(60^\circ)} = \sqrt{3}$$

ti84.instructions: Enter sin(420) and check if it equals √(3)/2 (approx 0.866). Repeat for cos(420) and tan(420).

desmos.instructions: Type sin(420 degrees), cos(420 degrees), tan(420 degrees)

calculator.description: Verify the values for the double angle

parts.content: Confirm that the identity $1+\tan^2(2\theta)=\sec^2(2\theta)$ holds true for the calculated double angle values.
parts.markscheme:
- LHS $= 1+(\sqrt{3})^2=4$. <marks>M1</marks>
- RHS $= \sec^2(2\theta)=\frac{1}{(1/2)^2}=4$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Retrieve calculated values
steps.content:
To confirm the identity, we use the double angle values for $\tan(2\theta)$ and $\cos(2\theta)$ that were found in the previous parts of the question. Based on the working shown in the markscheme, these values are:

$$\tan(2\theta) = \sqrt{3}$$
$$\cos(2\theta) = \frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Evaluate Left Hand Side
steps.content:
We calculate the numerical value of the Left Hand Side (LHS) of the identity $1+\tan^2(2\theta)$ by substituting $\tan(2\theta) = \sqrt{3}$:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= 1 + (\sqrt{3})^2 \\ &= 1 + 3 = 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Evaluate Right Hand Side
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the value of the Right Hand Side (RHS) $\sec^2(2\theta)$. Recall that secant is the reciprocal of cosine, so $\sec(x) = \frac{1}{\cos(x)}$.

Using $\cos(2\theta) = \frac{1}{2}$:

$$\sec(2\theta) = \frac{1}{\cos(2\theta)} = \frac{1}{1/2} = 2$$

Now, square this value to find the RHS:

$$\text{RHS} = (2)^2 = 4$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
Finally, we compare the results from both sides:

$$\text{LHS} = 4$$
$$\text{RHS} = 4$$

Since $\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$, the identity $1+\tan^2(2\theta)=\sec^2(2\theta)$ is confirmed for the calculated values.

steps.title: Identify known values
steps.content:
From previous parts of the question, we have determined the values for the sine and cosine of the double angle:

$$\sin(2\theta) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2} \quad \text{and} \quad \cos(2\theta) = \frac{1}{2}$$

steps.title: Convert LHS to sine/cosine
steps.content:
To verify the identity using the **Conversion to Sine and Cosine** approach, we first rewrite the left-hand side (LHS) in terms of $\sin$ and $\cos$. Recall that $\tan A = \frac{\sin A}{\cos A}$.

$$\text{LHS} = 1 + \tan^2(2\theta) = 1 + \left( \frac{\sin(2\theta)}{\cos(2\theta)} \right)^2$$

steps.title: Evaluate LHS
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values $\sin(2\theta) = \frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}$ and $\cos(2\theta) = \frac{1}{2}$ into the expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{LHS} &= 1 + \left( \frac{\frac{\sqrt{3}}{2}}{\frac{1}{2}} \right)^2 \\ &= 1 + (\sqrt{3})^2 \\ &= 1 + 3 \\ &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Convert RHS to cosine
steps.content:
Next, we rewrite the right-hand side (RHS) in terms of cosine. Recall that $\sec A = \frac{1}{\cos A}$.

$$\text{RHS} = \sec^2(2\theta) = \frac{1}{\cos^2(2\theta)}$$

steps.title: Evaluate RHS
steps.content:
Substitute the value $\cos(2\theta) = \frac{1}{2}$ into this expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{RHS} &= \frac{1}{\left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^2} \\ &= \frac{1}{\frac{1}{4}} \\ &= 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Confirm the identity
steps.content:
Comparing the results from both sides:

$$\text{LHS} = 4 \quad \text{and} \quad \text{RHS} = 4$$

Since $\text{LHS} = \text{RHS}$, the identity $1+\tan^2(2\theta)=\sec^2(2\theta)$ is confirmed for the calculated values.

parts.content:
The tracking precision $K$ is given by $K = (\cos\theta-\sin\theta)^2$.

Over a full turn ($0\le\theta<2\pi$), determine the **maximum** and **minimum** possible tracking precisions, giving exact values.
parts.markscheme:
- Simplify: $K = 1-\sin(2\theta)$. <marks>M1</marks>
- Max when $\sin(2\theta)=-1 \Rightarrow K_{\text{max}}=2$. <marks>A1</marks>
- Min when $\sin(2\theta)=1 \Rightarrow K_{\text{min}}=0$. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Expand the squared expression
steps.content:
First, we expand the expression for tracking precision $K$ by squaring the binomial term:

$$ K = (\cos\theta - \sin\theta)^2 $$

Using the algebraic identity $(a-b)^2 = a^2 - 2ab + b^2$, we get:

$$ K = \cos^2\theta - 2\cos\theta\sin\theta + \sin^2\theta $$

steps.title: Apply Pythagorean identity
steps.content:
We can rearrange the terms to group the squared components:

$$ K = (\cos^2\theta + \sin^2\theta) - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta $$

From the **Trigonometry** section of the data booklet, we apply the Pythagorean identity:

$$ K = 1 - 2\sin\theta\cos\theta $$

steps.title: Apply double angle identity
steps.content:
Next, we simplify the remaining term using the double angle identity for sine found in the data booklet:

$$ K = 1 - \sin(2\theta) $$

This simplified form makes it much easier to analyze the maximum and minimum values.

gdc.instructions: Calculate both expressions to verify they are equal.

ti84.instructions: Enter the original expression and the simplified expression to check they give the same result.

desmos.instructions: Type both expressions on separate lines to verify the outputs match.

calculator.description: Verify the simplification by checking a value (e.g., θ = 0.5 rad) in both the original and simplified formula.

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
The sine function $\sin(x)$ always oscillates between $-1$ and $1$. For the term $\sin(2\theta)$, the range is:

$$ -1 \le \sin(2\theta) \le 1 $$

Since our function is $K = 1 - \sin(2\theta)$, we evaluate the extrema:

*   **Maximum K** occurs when we subtract the smallest value ($-1$):
    $$ K_{\text{max}} = 1 - (-1) = 2 $$

*   **Minimum K** occurs when we subtract the largest value ($1$):
    $$ K_{\text{min}} = 1 - (1) = 0 $$

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
The maximum and minimum possible tracking precisions are:

**Maximum:** $2$
**Minimum:** $0$

steps.title: Differentiate using chain rule
steps.content:
To find the stationary points, we first find the derivative of $K$ with respect to $\theta$ using the chain rule.

Let $u = \cos\theta - \sin\theta$, so $K = u^2$.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dK}{d\theta} &= \frac{dK}{du} \times \frac{du}{d\theta} \\ &= 2(\cos\theta - \sin\theta) \times \frac{d}{d\theta}(\cos\theta - \sin\theta) \\ &= 2(\cos\theta - \sin\theta)(-\sin\theta - \cos\theta) \end{aligned}$$

highlights.text: Find the derivative of $K$ with respect to $	heta$ (using the chain rule)

steps.title: Simplify the derivative
steps.content:
We can simplify the expression by factoring out $-1$ from the second term and using the double angle identity.

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{dK}{d\theta} &= -2(\cos\theta - \sin\theta)(\cos\theta + \sin\theta) \\ &= -2(\cos^2\theta - \sin^2\theta) \end{aligned}$$

Using the double angle identity from the data booklet:
$$\frac{dK}{d\theta} = -2\cos(2\theta)$$

steps.title: Identify critical points
steps.content:
Set the derivative to zero to find the critical stationary points in the domain $0 \le \theta < 2\pi$.

$$-2\cos(2\theta) = 0 \implies \cos(2\theta) = 0$$

The solutions for $2\theta$ (where the domain is $0 \le 2\theta < 4\pi$) are:
$$2\theta = \frac{\pi}{2}, \frac{3\pi}{2}, \frac{5\pi}{2}, \frac{7\pi}{2}$$

Dividing by 2 gives our critical values for $\theta$:
$$\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}, \frac{3\pi}{4}, \frac{5\pi}{4}, \frac{7\pi}{4}$$

1. Go to Graph menu
2. Enter y1 = -2cos(2x)
3. Set View Window to 0 to 2pi
4. Use G-Solv > Root to find x-intercepts

2. Enter Y1 = -2cos(2X)
3. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=0, Xmax=2pi
4. Press [GRAPH]
5. Find zeros using [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 2:Zero

desmos.instructions:
1. Type f(x) = -2cos(2x)
2. Look for intersections with the x-axis between 0 and 2pi

highlights.text: set it to zero to identify critical stationary points

steps.title: Substitute to find values
steps.content:
Substitute these critical angles back into the original equation $K = (\cos\theta - \sin\theta)^2$ to find the maximum and minimum values.

For $\theta = \frac{\pi}{4}$ and $\frac{5\pi}{4}$ (where $\cos\theta = \sin\theta$):
$$K = \left(\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)^2 = 0$$

For $\theta = \frac{3\pi}{4}$ (where $\cos\theta = -\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}, \sin\theta = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}$):
$$K = \left(-\frac{\sqrt{2}}{2} - \frac{\sqrt{2}}{2}\right)^2 = (-\sqrt{2})^2 = 2$$

Similarly for $\theta = \frac{7\pi}{4}$, $K = (\sqrt{2})^2 = 2$.

ti84.instructions:
1. Return to home screen [2nd] [MODE] (QUIT)
2. Enter (cos(3π/4)-sin(3π/4))^2
3. Press [ENTER] to confirm result is 2

highlights.text: substitute these angles back into the original equation to find the maximum and minimum values

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Comparing the values calculated:

Maximum tracking precision: **2**
Minimum tracking precision: **0**

steps.title: Strategy: Harmonic Form
steps.content:
The question asks for the maximum and minimum values of the tracking precision $K$. To solve this, we can simplify the expression inside the square using the **Harmonic Form (R-method)**. 

We will combine the linear combination of sine and cosine into a single cosine function of the form:
$$R\cos(\theta + \alpha)$$

steps.title: Find the amplitude R
steps.content:
We compare the expression $\cos\theta - \sin\theta$ to the expansion of the harmonic form:
$$R\cos(\theta + \alpha) = R(\cos\theta\cos\alpha - \sin\theta\sin\alpha)$$

By equating coefficients ($1\cos\theta - 1\sin\theta$), we have $R\cos\alpha = 1$ and $R\sin\alpha = 1$. We calculate the amplitude $R$:
$$R = \sqrt{1^2 + 1^2} = \sqrt{2}$$

ti84.instructions: Calculate the square root of the sum of squares:

steps.title: Find the phase angle alpha
steps.content:
Next, we find the angle $\alpha$. Since both $\cos\alpha$ and $\sin\alpha$ are positive, $\alpha$ is in the first quadrant.
$$\tan\alpha = \frac{1}{1} = 1 \Rightarrow \alpha = \frac{\pi}{4}$$

Substituting $R$ and $\alpha$ back, we can rewrite the expression as:
$$\cos\theta - \sin\theta = \sqrt{2}\cos\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression for K
steps.content:
Now we substitute this single trigonometric ratio back into the equation for $K$ and square it:
$$\begin{aligned} K &= (\cos\theta - \sin\theta)^2 \\ &= \left(\sqrt{2}\cos\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right)\right)^2 \\ &= 2\cos^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine maximum and minimum
steps.content:
We know that for any real angle, the range of $\cos^2(x)$ is $[0, 1]$. Since the domain covers a full turn ($0 \le \theta < 2\pi$), the argument $\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}$ covers a full period, so the cosine function reaches all possible values.

*   **Maximum:** Occurs when $\cos^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 1$
    $$K_{\text{max}} = 2(1) = 2$$
*   **Minimum:** Occurs when $\cos^2\left(\theta + \frac{\pi}{4}\right) = 0$
    $$K_{\text{min}} = 2(0) = 0$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Graph y = (cos(x) - sin(x))^2
2. Use G-Solve (or Analyze Graph) > Max/Min
3. Set domain 0 to 2π

4. Press [GRAPH]
5. Use [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) to find minimum and maximum

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The maximum possible tracking precision is **2** and the minimum is **0**.

parts.markscheme:
